aircraft.name: Fiat CR.42 Falco
aircraft.notes: The last major biplane fighter to see combat, the CR.42 was Italy's frontline fighter at war's start. Outclassed by monoplane opponents, it transitioned to ground-attack and night harassment roles.

aircraft.name: Macchi C.202 Folgore
aircraft.engine: Alfa Romeo RA.1000 RC.41 (Daimler-Benz DB 601A licence), 1,175 hp
aircraft.notes: Italy's best mass-produced fighter of the war. Performance matched contemporaries, but chronic armament and engine shortages limited its impact.

aircraft.name: Mitsubishi A6M Zero
aircraft.notes: The dominant Allied opponent in the Pacific 1941–42. Exceptional range and manoeuvrability, but lack of pilot armour and self-sealing fuel tanks made it vulnerable once Allied tactics adapted.

aircraft.name: Nakajima Ki-43 Hayabusa
aircraft.notes: The Imperial Japanese Army's primary fighter for most of the war. Highly manoeuvrable but lightly armed and vulnerable; pilots called it 'Peregrine Falcon'.

aircraft.name: Kawasaki Ki-61 Hien
aircraft.notes: Japan's only liquid-cooled engine fighter of the war. Mistaken by Allied pilots for a Bf 109. Engine reliability was poor.

aircraft.name: IAR 80
aircraft.notes: Romania's indigenous fighter design, competitive at its introduction. Used effectively on the Eastern Front defending Romanian oil fields at Ploiești.
